results.content:
# Council of Models — Navigation Cohesion & A11y Advisory ## Dashboard Cross-Page Architecture Review --- ## ROUND 1: OPENING POSITIONS --- ### Model A — Architect (Systems & Build Tooling) **On Question 1 — Architecture Pattern** For a single-developer static-HTML build via `node scripts/build-dashboard.mjs`, **build-time component injection (option a)** is unambiguously correct. The build script already exists. You extend it to read a `_partials/nav.html` file and inject it at a defined slot (e.g., a `` comment or a `` custom element) into every page before write. This is the pattern used by GOV.UK's Nunjucks/Eleventy pipeline and the US Web Design System's Jekyll build — both of which serve as reference implementations for multi-page a11y. Client-side injection (option b) creates a flash of missing navigation — the nav doesn't exist in the DOM on paint, creating a layout shift (CLS) and a period where keyboard users who Tab early find no nav landmarks. It also violates the principle that assistive technology should be able to read landmark structure on first parse. The WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices 1.1, §3.4 (Navigation Landmark) explicitly frames nav as a structural element that defines page geography — it must exist in the initial DOM tree. Iframe (option c) is the worst option. The nav lives in a separate browsing context, meaning keyboard focus crosses browsing context boundaries. The iframe's `
` is invisible to the parent page's landmark navigation (VoiceOver/NVDA `F6` landmark scan). It fails WCAG 2.1 **SC 1.3.6 Identify Purpose** (AA) and creates a maintenance horror. ServiceWorker injection (option d) is architecturally interesting but semantically wrong here. SW intercepts network requests — injecting HTML via SW means either you're rewriting response streams (complex, fragile) or you're doing it on the client after fetch (same problems as option b, with added indirection). The service-worker.js already present is presumably for offline caching; repurposing it for nav injection creates a debugging surface where nav failures are invisible in DevTools HTML panel. `

**Round 1: opening positions** **Alpha (Architecture & Performance):** For architecture (1), build-time component injection (1a) via `scripts/build-dashboard.mjs` is the undisputed king for a static multi-page app (MPA). We read a `nav.partial.html` and inject it into `index.html`, `contacts.html`, and `network-database.html`. Zero client-side JS parsing for core routing, maximum resilience. For performance (10), rendering a shared nav across a 65,000-line contacts page costs virtually nothing if we use an external cached `dashboard.css` and an SVG sprite (``) injected at the bottom of the body. **Beta (Accessibility & WCAG Compliance):** Regardless of how it's built, ARIA landmarks (5) are mandatory per WCAG 1.3.1 (Info and Relationships). The minimum set for every page must be: - `
` (contains brand/skip-link) - `
` - `
` For skip-links (4), WCAG 2.4.1 (Bypass Blocks) requires them to be the first focusable element. On `index.html` and `network-database.html`, it lands on `
`. On `contacts.html`, it should land directly on the search input (``), as that is the primary functional intent of the page. **Gamma (UX & Mental Models):** Mitchell’s current setup mixes in-page anchors, modals, and a single cross-page link in one left sidebar. This violates standard mental models. For the nav pattern (2), I propose a Hybrid approach (2c): a Top Horizontal Toolbar for *global* routing (Dashboard, Contacts, Network DB), leaving the Left Sidebar on `index.html` as a *local* in-page filter/anchor menu. This matches the US Web Design System (USWDS) "App Shell" pattern. For the active-page indicator (3), apply `aria-current="page"` to the active global link, styled visually with a heavy `border-bottom` (WCAG 1.4.1 Use of Color requires we don't use color alone; shape/border is required). **Round 2: dissent** **Beta (Accessibility & WCAG Compliance):** I strongly dissent against Gamma's dual-nav (Top + Left) proposal. Splitting navigation into two separate axes introduces cognitive load and risks violating WCAG 3.2.3 (Consistent Navigation). If a user accesses the dashboard on a 13-inch laptop, a top global nav plus a 200px left local nav wastes viewport space. Furthermore, regarding focus management on page transition (7), since this is an MPA, the browser natively handles focus on load (putting it at the top of the document). We do not need a JS-based focus manager, but the skip-link *must* remain visible on `:focus` (WCAG 2.4.7 Focus Visible). **Gamma (UX & Mental Models):** Beta, keeping a unified persistent left sidebar (2a) for everything means 9 items on `contacts.html` will do nothing but bounce the user *back* to the dashboard, or worse, they act as absolute URLs (`/#overview-section`) that force a hard reload. But I concede a single UI paradigm is better for muscle memory. Alpha, I dissent on build-time injection for personal tools. If Mitchell wants to add a quick link, he has to run a Node script that rebuilds a 65,000-line HTML file. That's terrible DX. **Alpha (Architecture & Performance):** Gamma, build-time injection is literally sub-100ms in Node via `fs.readFileSync` and `String.prototype.replace()`. It's trivial. However, I want to address keyboard shortcuts (6). For an INTJ AI builder, a global keyboard shortcut layer (e.g., `g h` for home, `g c` for contacts) modeled after Linear or GitHub is not scope creep; it’s a high-ROI velocity multiplier. We wrap it in a lightweight ``. Use an inline SVG sprite (``) stored in `_nav.html` to eliminate external image requests and reduce DOM bloat on the massive `contacts.html` file. CSS should be an external, heavily cached `dashboard.css` file containing all structural and token-based styles.

- **Round 1: opening positions** **Model A — Architecture** Best fit for this repo is **(a) build-time component injection**: generate a shared header/nav partial into every HTML page at build time from `node scripts/build-dashboard.mjs`. Why: it gives the strongest WCAG reliability because the nav exists in the initial HTML, works without JS, survives script failure, and keeps `index.html`, `contacts.html`, and `network-database.html` synchronized by construction. This is the most robust choice for a static-HTML-output dashboard. Why not the others: - **(b) client-side nav injection**: fragile for accessibility because the landmark/nav may appear late or fail if JS errors; also creates a focus/route timing burden. - **(c) iframe header**: poor fit; cross-document focus/landmark behavior is worse, and it complicates keyboard and screen-reader navigation. - **(d) ServiceWorker-injected nav**: not appropriate for primary document structure; SWs should cache/respond, not mutate critical navigation markup. - **(e) `
